0.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for an initial consult regarding your breast cancer treatment.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. It is also estrogen receptor positive (ER+) and progesterone receptor positive (PR+), and does not have a protein called HER2.
Your cancer was originally at an early stage (Stage IIA) but has now spread to other parts of your body, making it advanced stage (Stage IV).
**What tests were done or ordered?**
A CT scan showed that the cancer has spread to your lungs, abdomen, and ovaries. You also have a lump in your armpit area that needs to be checked.
You will have a brain MRI and a bone scan to check for any other areas where the cancer might have spread.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
If the biopsy shows that the cancer is still ER+ and does not have HER2, you may be given a medication called Ibrance.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You are referred to Integrative Medicine for natural therapies.
You will have a biopsy of the lump in your armpit to confirm the type of cancer.
You will have a follow-up visit after the results of the tests are complete so we can make a treatment plan.
Your advance care status is noted as Full code.
We understand that you are feeling anxious and distressed. Please know that your husband is a great support and we are here to help you through this.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

1.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a follow-up visit regarding your breast cancer treatment.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called triple negative invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts and does not respond to certain hormones or proteins. The cancer has now spread to other parts of your body, making it advanced stage (Stage IV).
The treatment is aimed at relieving symptoms and improving your quality of life.
Your back pain has gotten worse, which might be due to the cancer spreading. You also have low levels of red blood cells, which can make you feel tired.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
Your blood tests show that you have low levels of several important minerals and proteins, including albumin, calcium, chloride, potassium, and sodium. Your liver function tests also show some changes.
You will have more scans in 3 months to check how the cancer is doing. If your headaches get worse, you may need a brain scan.
Your doctor will also check your liver function and hepatitis B status every 4 months.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
Your irinotecan treatment schedule has been changed to every other week, and the dose has been increased.
You are also taking several medications to help with nausea, pain, and diarrhea.
You will receive a medication to treat an infection, and you will have a blood transfusion to help with your low red blood cell count.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You are referred to a radiation oncologist to discuss treatment options for your pain.
You will have a follow-up visit in 2 weeks.
You may also be referred to social work and home health services for additional support.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel

14.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a second opinion regarding your newly diagnosed left breast cancer. The cancer is a mix of two types: one that started in the milk-producing glands and one that started in the milk ducts. You wanted to discuss your diagnosis, what might happen in the future, and the different treatment options.
**What was discussed?**
The cancer is in an early stage, based on imaging and exam. It has not spread to other parts of your body. The goal of treatment is to cure the cancer.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
An ultrasound was done to look at the cancer in your breast. No blood tests were done or ordered.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
If you choose to start medication treatment, a plan called a combination of chemotherapy and targeted therapy drugs would be favored. No other changes were made to your current medications.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You are referred to breast surgery for further treatment. Your next visit will be in person to see your doctor formally later today. After your surgery, the final treatment plan will be based on the final pathology results.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

***** ***** is a 53 year old female with breast cancer who is here for a consultation regarding further management    SUBJECTIVE    HPI    ***** recently presented with a palpable mass in the right breast. The workup was negative for the right breast but picked up a left breast cancer. This is summarized below.    July 2021 - bilateral 3D mammography = left breast spiculated mass 8mm, no correlate to the palpable right breast mass  July 2021 - bilateral breast US = suspicious left breast mass, no correlate to the palpable right breast mass  August 2021 - *****-guided left breast biopsy = invasive ductal ca, grade 1-2; ER positive (strong, >95%), PR positive (strong, >95%), ***** negative (IHC 0; FISH 2.June 09.5=1.1X), *****=5%.  November 2021 - s.p left lumpectomy & *****  ***** = invasive ductal ca, 0.8cm, grade 2, ***** negative, margins negative, no DCIS, LNs negative (0/5).     Other recent data  August 2021 - Pelvic US = negative, s/p hysterectomy,. Normal ovaries with left corpus luteum  August 2021 - Chest CT = negative    ***** has recovered from her surgery and other than some minor post-operative numbness, she is essentially asymptomatic.     *****'s menopausal status is difficult to know. She is amenorrheic due to prior hysterectomy and reports rare hot flashes.     PMH  h/o depression & anxiety  h/o fibroids  s/p excision of large lipoma from back 2016  s/p lap hysterectomy and right salpingectomy 2019 for 14cm large fibroid      Meds  none    Allergies  Amoxicillin, erythromycin, Bactrim - the connection is difficult to know. It dates back to a single episode in her 20s when she was on ***** ***** ***** for UTI and the link remains unknown.    Social History  Lives in ***** *****. Single, no kids. She is a neuromuscular massage therapist but hasn't worked since surgery. She is planning on going back to office work. She quit smoking in 2006. Does not drink EtOH.    Family History  Sister with ovarian ca @40s  Maternal aunt with 
 H&N ca @60  Maternal uncle with colon ca @56  Maternal grandfather with prostate ***** @90  Paternal aunt with breast ca @60s  Her father's family is estranged and she doesn't know much about them      OBJECTIVE    This is a video visit    IMPRESSION    ***** pathologic prognostic stage ***** (*****) left breast cancer, ER/PR positive, ***** negative    PLAN    Status of disease and management recommendations were discussed at length with *****. She has completed the surgical management of her left breast cancer. I estimate that without further systemic therapy she has an approximately 10-15% chance of recurrence. This risk can be reduced with adjuvant endocrine therapy. ***** will benefit from adjuvant hormonal therapy for a duration of at least 5 years. Both tamoxifen and AIs are effective with AIs showing superior efficacy in randomized trials (***** ***** *****)(***** ***** *****). Side effects of tamoxifen were discussed including potential for hot flashes, mood effects, increased incidence of thromboembolic events and endometrial cancer in postmenopausal women. AIs are restricted to the post-menopausal state and their side effects were discussed including *****, myalgias, and potential acceleration of bone loss. *****'s menopausal status is difficult to know. We will check hormone levels and make a recommendation for tamoxifen or ***** based on her menopausal status.     ***** requires breast radiotherapy to complete her breast conservation management. She is scheduled to see ***** tomorrow.     *****'s family history raises some suspicion of an inherited susceptibility syndrome. ***** refer to genetics for further evaluation and testing. I will try to ***** this, as a positive test may lead her to exercise the option of prophylactic mastectomy, and thus radiotherapy will have to be postponed until she has test results.    In summary the plan is:  - check labs including hormones  - baseline DXA scan  - 
 ***** consult tomorrow  - referral to genetics  - referral to nutritionist at her request  - F/U visit after RT to start endocrine therapy based on serologic determination of menopausal status

Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a consultation regarding further management after surgical treatment for breast cancer.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. It is ER and PR positive, which means the cancer cells have proteins that can be affected by hormones, and it is HER2 negative.
The cancer has not spread to other parts of your body.
The goal of treatment is to cure the cancer.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
Your blood tests are mostly normal.
You will need to have a bone density scan.
You will also have some blood tests to check your hormone levels.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
You will need to have radiation therapy to the breast to complete your treatment.
You will be recommended to take a hormone therapy medication for at least 5 years. The type of medication will depend on whether you are in menopause or not.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You are referred to a specialist for radiation therapy.
You are also referred to genetics for further testing.
You will see a nutritionist as you requested.
You will have your hormone levels checked to decide on the best hormone therapy for you.
You will have a follow-up visit after your radiation therapy to start the hormone therapy.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a first-time visit regarding a newly found cancer in your left breast.
**What was discussed?**
The cancer in your left breast is a type that started in the milk ducts (invasive ductal carcinoma). It is likely in an advanced stage because it is large and has spread to the lymph nodes under your arm. The main goal of treatment is to try to cure the cancer.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
Tests showed a lump in your left breast and that the cancer has spread to the lymph nodes under your arm. No cancer was found in other parts of your body. You will need an echocardiogram before starting your treatment.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
You will start a treatment called neoadjuvant chemotherapy. This means you will have chemotherapy before surgery to try to shrink the cancer. The treatment plan is called a combination of chemotherapy and targeted therapy drugs.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You will need to have a port placed for your chemotherapy. This is a small device put under your skin to make it easier to give you the medicine. You will also need an echocardiogram to check your heart before starting the treatment.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

HPI   ***** felt a lump in left breast this ***** and sought medical attention. This began in ***** where she resides, and subsequently came to UCSF for surgery. The management is summarized below.    October 2016 -3D mammogram = left breast 15mm mass with Ca+ at 2O'*****;  right breast negative' heterogeneously dense breasts  October 2016 - left breast US = irregular mass 2.2x1.7cm at *****  December 2016 - Left breast core biopsy = invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 2-3, ER positive (95%), PR weak (5%), ***** negative (IHC 1+)     03/01/2017 - s/p left lumpectomy & SLN Bx  ***** = invasive ductal ca with neuroendocrine differentiation, 4.5cm, grade 3, ***** extensively positive, margins negative in the additional slices. ER positive (>95%), PR positive (30%), ***** positive (IHC heterogeneous with IHC 2+/3+ in 90:10% regions; FISH 4.9X). *****=25%.  Also DCIS int/high grade spanning 4.5cm. Also ***** positive in a heterogenous 2+/3+ pattern. SLN positive (May 02) for 6mm met.    She has recovered well from surgery, and other than some minor post-operative symptoms, she is essentially asymptomatic and has an excellent performance status.    PMH - benign    Meds - none    Allergies - NKDA    Social History  ***** lives in *****. She is divorced with one child. She works in elementary school and also a waitress. She has never smoked and only rarely uses EtOH. Lymphadenopathy:     She has no cervical adenopathy.     She has no axillary adenopathy.        Right: No supraclavicular adenopathy present.        Left: No supraclavicular adenopathy present. Assessment:       Stage II/III left breast cancer, ER/PR positive, ***** positive, s/p left lumpectomy/*****      Plan:       Status of disease and 
 management recommendations were discussed at length with ***** and her two sisters who were present. ***** has undergone the surgical resection of her left breast tumor with SLN assessment as well. It is likely that she has additional LNs with metastatic disease. This is estimated to be about 64% by the MSKCC normogram and 44% by the ***** normogram. The role of axillary dissection and its pros/cons have been discussed with her by Dr. *****. ***** remains with a considerable risk of systemic relapse and mortality from this cancer. Although the most accurate risk assessment is not possible without quantitative LN data from an axillary dissection, her risk is clearly high. I estimate her risk to be on the order of 60% or higher. This can be reduced with the use of adjuvant chemotherapy, *****-targeting therapy. hormonal therapy, radiotherapy, and bisphosphonate therapy. These modalities were discussed.    ***** will benefit from adjuvant systemic chemotherapy. Recommendation was made for adjuvant AC/THP chemotherapy. This consists of AC x 4 given q2weeks with growth factor support, followed by 12 weeks of taxol along with one year of trastuzumab and pertuzumab. Side effects were discussed including potential for nausea/vomiting, alopecia, bone marrow suppression and infections, cardiomyopathy, neuropathy, and a small risk of secondary leukemias. As an alternative, we also discussed the TCHP regimen. This consists of 6 cycles of TCHP with one year of trastuzumab/pertuzumab. Side effects are similar with sparing of the cardiomyopathy and leukemogenic risks.     We also discussed the use of extended adjuvant neratinib in year 2 including the potential for diarrhea and the need for prophylactic anti-*****.     ***** will also benefit from adjuvant hormone therapy. A recommendation was made for Arimidex 1 mg PO daily for a duration of 10 years. This would begin at the completion of chemotherapy. Side effects were discussed 
 including the potential for ***** and myalgias, as well as acceleration of bone loss and associated complications including bone fractures. In the case of intolerable arthralgias or myalgias, treatment can be changed to other aromatase inhibitors or possibly to tamoxifen. Bone loss can be monitored by densitometry scanning and can be reduced or reversed with a number of bone strengthening agents available for this purpose. We also discussed the use of ***** infusions to maintain bone density as well as reduce the recurrence risk associated with breast cancer.    ***** requires adjuvant breast radiotherapy to complete her breast conservation management. Referral will be made to ***** at the completion of chemotherapy for a consultation and further management.    We discussed her family history and the low likelihood of an inherited susceptibility. I offered a referral to genetic counseling for further evaluation.     ***** has significant concerns about the toxicities associated with these therapies. She will be thinking about these options over the next few weeks. She also plans to return to *****, and if she is to undergo adjuvant chemotherapy, she would want to get it on the island of ***** where she lives. She also has concerns about co-pays that may be associated with treatment and her ability to work, disability options, and other practical considerations. She will be thinking about and investigating these issues over the next couple of weeks and will get back to us. We will send records to ***** when she has identified a local oncologist.

52.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a consultation regarding your recently diagnosed stage II/III left breast cancer.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. It also has neuroendocrine differentiation, which means it has some features of cells that make hormones. Additionally, you have a condition called DCIS, which is when abnormal cells are found in the lining of a breast duct.
The goal of your treatment is to cure the cancer.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
No new tests were done during this visit.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
You were recommended to start adjuvant chemotherapy, which is treatment given after surgery to prevent the cancer from coming back. The options discussed were AC/targeted therapy with chemotherapy or a combination of chemotherapy and targeted therapy drugs. Both involve several cycles of chemotherapy drugs. You were also recommended to take a medication called Arimidex for 10 years to help prevent the cancer from coming back.
You may also need to take other medications to help with bone health and to prevent the cancer from spreading.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You will need to have radiation therapy to the breast after your chemotherapy is done.
You are referred to a specialist for a consultation on radiation therapy after your chemotherapy is completed.
You were offered a referral to genetic counseling to check if your cancer might be due to inherited factors.
You will think about these treatment options over the next few weeks and get back to us with your decision.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

We next discussed treatment.  In general it has been thought that neoadjuvant chemotherapy may offer the opportunity to downstage a tumor prior to resection and thus facilitate breast conservation, with no difference in ***** or OS vs ***** surgery. However, new evidence is emerging regarding the efficacy of adjuvant ***** targeted therapy in the setting of residual disease after neoadjuvant treatment (***** 2019;380:617-28), with improved DFS and a trend towards improved OS. Therefore, we recommend neoadjuvant treatment.    There are multiple reasonable regimens, although we suggest TCHP (docetaxel and carboplatin every 3 weeks for 6 cycles with concurrent ***** and pertuzumab) or ***** (weekly paclitaxel with carboplatin, administered every three weeks or weekly, with concurrent trastuzumab and pertuzumab). Adjuvant treatment selection will be guided by an assessment of tumor response at resection, including the potential option ***** cycles of *****-***** emtansine if there is not a complete response to neoadjuvant therapy.    We briefly discussed treatment side effects including hair loss, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, and risk of infection, but left a full discussion for formal chemotherapy teaching.    We also discussed the need for radiation therapy and surgical resection. We will refer Ms. ***** to the UCSF Breast Surgery clinic for consultation regarding surgical resection.  Following surgical resection we will plan to refer Ms. ***** to Radiation Oncology.

74.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a second opinion regarding your breast cancer treatment.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. The cancer is also HER2 positive, which means it has a protein that can make the cancer grow faster.
The goal of your treatment is to cure the cancer.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
A mammogram and a PET/CT scan were done to check the size and spread of the cancer. The tests showed a 2.1cm mass in your right breast and some swollen lymph nodes in your armpit.
A TTE (echocardiogram) will be done before you start chemotherapy.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
You will start a treatment called neoadjuvant a combination of chemotherapy and targeted therapy drugs. This treatment includes chemotherapy to shrink the cancer before surgery.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You are referred to UCSF Breast Surgery for a consultation about your surgery.
You are also referred to Radiation Oncology after your surgery.
You will have genetic counseling and fertility referrals.
We will wait for the results of your genetic testing before planning your next steps.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

We had a lengthy discussion with the patient and her daughter which we reviewed her breast cancer diagnosis, workup and treatment history to date. I reviewed all of the relevant and available clinic notes, imaging, and pathology reports. First, we reviewed *****'s pathology confirming that she has metastatic breast cancer which is estrogen receptor positive 
 and ***** negative based on core needle biopsy of an axillary LN in November 2019. She presented with extensive lytic bone lesions at that time. She has since developed metastatic disease to the liver and pachymeninges and possibly to the leptomeninges, although her first CSF cytology was negative for malignant cells. We explained that metastatic breast cancer is not curable, but it is treatable and there are therapies that will extend the quality and duration of life.     We reviewed her treatment course to date. She was initially started on letrozole/palbociclib in December 2019, but this was discontinued due to PD to the liver and LN in July 2020. WE assured ***** that this was an appropriate treatment choice as initial therapy of *****+/*****- metastatic disease. Upon progression, she was switched to Xeloda (initially 500mg BID inadvertantly, then 1000mg BID in mid-September, and 1500 mg BID since late September). We tried to explain to the patient and family the difference between ***** based/***** disease and leptomeningeal disease and stressed that it can be very difficulty radiographically to distinguish the two. While her first CSF cytology was negative for malignant cells, her radiographic and clinical findings raise suspicion for leptomeningeal disease.    Recently, her neurologic symptoms are getting progressively worse, with no improvement in her vision and now auditory symptoms. Despite her initial CSF cytology being negative, we explained that  false negatives are not uncommon and we recommend repeat LP with cytology now. We also recommend referral to 
 radiation oncology for consideration of radiation therapy (either just to her internal auditory canal or to a larger portion of her *****). We would also consider starting low dose steroids to see if this improves her symptoms.     In terms of future options, if a CT CAP shows clear progression on Xeloda, we would consider another hormone-based therapy strategy. We would consider fulvestrant plus the ***** inhibitor ***** (*****) (***** ***** 2019), given her known ***** mutation on Strata testing. In this study, in the cohort of patients with *****-mutated cancer, progression-free survival at a median follow-up of 20 months was 11.0 months in the *****-fulvestrant group, as compared with 5.7 months in the placebo-fulvestrant group. However, this regimen does not have great CNS penetration, but there are few regimens that do.    Given that she has metastatic disease to the bone, we agree with continuing zolendronic acid    Recommendations:  -- Repeat CT CAP now  -- Repeat LP for CSF cytology  -- Repeat MRI spine to r/o LMD in the spine  -- Recommend referral to radiation oncology to consider either focal CNS radiation or possible even *****. The patient is interested in a UCSF Rad Onc opinion as well.  -- Could consider starting low dose steroids to see if reduction of swelling improves any of her symptoms  -- Continue xeloda 1500mg BID for now  -- At time of progression of disease, could consider fulvestrant + ***** given the ***** mutation on STRATA testing  -- Continue zolendronic acid    Patient seen and discussed with breast oncology attending *****. ***** *****.    ***** *****, MD PGY-5  Hematology/Oncology Fellow

83.letter:
Dear Patient,
We hope you are doing well. We are writing this letter to help you understand what happened during your recent visit.
**Why did I come to the clinic?**
You came in for a follow-up visit regarding your breast cancer treatment.
**What was discussed?**
You have a type of breast cancer called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means the cancer started in the milk ducts. The cancer has spread to multiple parts of your body, including your bones, liver, and possibly the lining of your brain.
The goal of your treatment is to manage your symptoms and improve your quality of life.
Recent tests show that the cancer is growing in some areas, even though you are on a new treatment called capecitabine.
**What tests were done or ordered?**
You had an MRI of your brain and a CT scan of your chest, abdomen, and pelvis. These tests showed that the cancer has grown in your liver and possibly in your brain.
You will need to have another CT scan of your chest, abdomen, and pelvis, an MRI of your spine, and a test called a lumbar puncture to check the fluid around your brain and spinal cord.
**What treatment or medication changes were made?**
You are now taking a medicine called capecitabine. You will continue this treatment for now.
You may also be given a low dose of steroids to help with swelling.
You will continue to take a medicine called zolendronic acid to help with your bone health.
You are referred to a radiation oncologist to consider radiation treatment for your brain.
**What is the plan going forward?**
You will have more tests to check how the cancer is doing. If the cancer continues to grow, you may be given a different treatment.
Your advance care status was not discussed during this visit.
Thank you for trusting us with your care.
Please feel free to contact us if you have any questions.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

Extensive osseous metastatic disease involving the axial/appendicular skeleton with old lower lumbar spine and left inferior pubic ramus pathologic fracture deformities. Physical exam showed right eye droop, otherwise no significant changes. No new palpable masses, skin changes, or lymphadenopathy noted.

findings: Recently, her neurologic symptoms are getting progressively worse, with no improvement in her vision and now auditory symptoms.

sentences.text: You will need to have another CT scan of your chest, abdomen, and pelvis, an MRI of your spine, and a test called a lumbar puncture to check the fluid around your brain and spinal cord.
